title: Ideas for Practicing Describing

pages.text:
58
Mindfulness Handout 4b (Mindfulness Worksheets 2–2c, 4–4b)
Ideas for Practicing Describing
Practice describing what you see outside of yourself: 1.		Lie on the ground and watch the clouds in the sky. Find and describe cloud patterns that
you see. 2.		Sit on a bench on a busy street or at a park. Describe one thing about each person who
walks by you. 3.		Find things in nature—a leaf, a drop of water, a pet or other animal. Describe each thing in
as much detail as you can. 4.		Describe as accurately as you can what a person has just said to you. Check to see if you
are correct. 5.		Describe a person’s face when the person seems angry, afraid, or sad. Notice and describe
the shape, movement, and placement of the forehead, eyebrows, and eyes; the lips and
mouth; the cheeks; and so on. 6.		Describe what a person has done or is doing now. Be very specific. Avoid describing
intentions or outcomes of the behavior that you do not directly observe. Avoid judgmental
language. 7.	Other:
Practice describing thoughts and feelings: 8.		Describe your feelings as they arise within you: “A feeling of anger is arising within me.” 9.		Describe your thoughts when you feel a strong emotion: “I feel X, and my thoughts are Y.”
10.		Describe your feelings after someone else does or says something: “When you do X, I feel
Y.”
11. 	Describe thoughts, feelings, and what you observed others do: “When you do X, I feel Y,
and my thoughts are Z.” “When X occurs, I feel Y, and my thoughts are Z.”
12.		Describe as many of your thoughts as you can while feeling a strong emotion.
13.	Other:
Practice describing your breathing:
14.		Each time you inhale and exhale, as you inhale, be aware that “I am inhaling, 1.” When you
exhale, be aware that “I am exhaling, 1.” Remember to breathe from the stomach. When
beginning the second inhalation, be aware that “I am inhaling, 2.” And, slowly exhaling, be
aware that “I am exhaling, 2.” Continue on up through 10. After you have reached 10, return
to 1. Whenever you lose count, return to 1.
15.		Begin to inhale gently and normally (from the stomach), describing in your mind that “I
am inhaling normally.” Exhale in awareness, “I am exhaling normally.” Continue for three
breaths. On the fourth breath, extend the inhalation, describing in your mind that “I am
breathing in a long inhalation.” Exhale in awareness, “I am breathing out a long exhalation.”
Continue for three breaths.
16.		Follow the entrance and exit of air. Say to yourself, “I am inhaling and following the
inhalation from its beginning to its end. I am exhaling and following the exhalation from its
beginning to its end.”
17. Other:

full_text:
Mindfulness Handout 4b (Mindfulness Worksheets 2–2c, 4–4b)
Ideas for Practicing Describing
Practice describing what you see outside of yourself: 1.		Lie on the ground and watch the clouds in the sky. Find and describe cloud patterns that
you see. 2.		Sit on a bench on a busy street or at a park. Describe one thing about each person who
walks by you. 3.		Find things in nature—a leaf, a drop of water, a pet or other animal. Describe each thing in
as much detail as you can. 4.		Describe as accurately as you can what a person has just said to you. Check to see if you
are correct. 5.		Describe a person’s face when the person seems angry, afraid, or sad. Notice and describe
the shape, movement, and placement of the forehead, eyebrows, and eyes; the lips and
mouth; the cheeks; and so on. 6.		Describe what a person has done or is doing now. Be very specific. Avoid describing
intentions or outcomes of the behavior that you do not directly observe. Avoid judgmental
language. 7.	Other:
Practice describing thoughts and feelings: 8.		Describe your feelings as they arise within you: “A feeling of anger is arising within me.” 9.		Describe your thoughts when you feel a strong emotion: “I feel X, and my thoughts are Y.”
10.		Describe your feelings after someone else does or says something: “When you do X, I feel
Y.”
11. 	Describe thoughts, feelings, and what you observed others do: “When you do X, I feel Y,
and my thoughts are Z.” “When X occurs, I feel Y, and my thoughts are Z.”
12.		Describe as many of your thoughts as you can while feeling a strong emotion.
13.	Other:
Practice describing your breathing:
14.		Each time you inhale and exhale, as you inhale, be aware that “I am inhaling, 1.” When you
exhale, be aware that “I am exhaling, 1.” Remember to breathe from the stomach. When
beginning the second inhalation, be aware that “I am inhaling, 2.” And, slowly exhaling, be
aware that “I am exhaling, 2.” Continue on up through 10. After you have reached 10, return
to 1. Whenever you lose count, return to 1.
15.		Begin to inhale gently and normally (from the stomach), describing in your mind that “I
am inhaling normally.” Exhale in awareness, “I am exhaling normally.” Continue for three
breaths. On the fourth breath, extend the inhalation, describing in your mind that “I am
breathing in a long inhalation.” Exhale in awareness, “I am breathing out a long exhalation.”
Continue for three breaths.
16.		Follow the entrance and exit of air. Say to yourself, “I am inhaling and following the
inhalation from its beginning to its end. I am exhaling and following the exhalation from its
beginning to its end.”
17. Other:

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Ideas for Practicing Describing

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Name one body sensation or one fact.
Choose one kind next step or stop for rest.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
